rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RAYMOND C. LOW AND IRENE C. LOW REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JULY 6, 2001
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Quash, Or In The Alternative Modify, The Subpoena For Production Of Business Records Issued To Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Respondent has demonstrated that the threatened intrusion into his privacy is serious. As stated in Burrows v. Superior Court (1974) 13 Cal.3d 238, 247, "[i]n the course of [dealing with banks], a depositor reveals many aspects of his personal affairs, opinions, habits and associations. Indeed, the totality of bank records provides a virtual current biography." Therefore, Respondent has satisfied the third prong of the Hill test. Petitioner alleges that disclosure of Respondent's personal financial records is "necessary to show the complete picture of how [Respondent] (mis)handled [the Settlor's] assets." (Opposition to Gary Low's Motion to Quash, or in the Alternative Modify, the Subpoena for Production of Business Records Issued to Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. [hereafter the "Opposition Brief"] at p. 2:1-2.) Petitioner has not shown that Respondent's interest in privacy is outweighed by Petitioner's interest such that the disclosure of more than 16 years' worth of Respondent's personal financial records is warranted. However, Petitioner has shown that disclosure of Respondent's financial records between the limited time period of May 8, 2018, to August 3, 2023, is warranted because Respondent admitted that he used his personal Wells Fargo bank account ending in -6281 for financial transactions he conducted while serving in a fiduciary capacity (Opposition Brief, Exhibit C, at p. 2:7-10; and Exhibit D, at p. 2:1-5). Respondent's request to quash Petitioner's Subpoena in its entirety is DENIED. Respondent's request to modify Petitioner's Subpoena is GRANTED. Petitioner's Subpoena is hereby modified to limit the requested period from May 8, 2018, to August 3, 2023. The prevailing party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. (part 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RAYMOND C. LOW AND IRENE C. LOW REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JULY 6, 2001
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash, Or In The Alternative Modify, The Subpoena For Production Of Business Records Issued To Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Respondent Gary Low (hereafter "Respondent")'s Motion to Quash, or in the Alternative Modify, the Subpoena for Production of Business Records issued to Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (hereafter the "Motion"). The Motion is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part for the reason set forth below. Pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 1987.1, the court may make an order quashing a subpoena entirely, modifying it, or directing compliance with it upon those terms or conditions as the court shall declare. On June 13, 2024, Petitioner Joseph Wu (hereafter "Petitioner") issued a Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records to Wells Fargo Bank, National Association, seeking "[a]ny and all bank and/or financial statements, reports, deposit slips, deposit items, withdrawal slip, canceled checks, cashier's check purchases, records of all types of electronic payments or deposits made relating to Gary Low's Wells Fargo Account ending in 6281, from January 1, 2008 to the present date" (hereafter "Petitioner's Subpoena"). In response, Respondent brought the instant Motion asserting his right to privacy and seeking to quash or modify Petitioner's Subpoena. The California Supreme Court established the framework for evaluating potential invasions of privacy in Hill v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn. (1997) 7 Cal.4th 1, 35. In short, the Hill test prescribes a mechanism for balancing the privacy interest at issue against the legitimate and important countervailing interest that would be served by disclosure. First, the party resisting disclosure must establish: (1) a legally protected privacy interest; (2) an objectively reasonable expectation of privacy in the given circumstances; and (3) a threatened intrusion that is serious. (Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 552.) In response, the party seeking disclosure may raise whatever legitimate and important countervailing interests would be served by disclosure, while the party seeking protection may identify feasible alternatives that serve the same interests or protective measures that would diminish the loss of privacy. (Ibid.) Respondent has established a legally protected privacy interest in the financial records sought by Petitioner. A bank customer's right of privacy in their financial records is "constitutionally founded." (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Court (1975) 15 Cal.3d 652, 655; See also Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3Cal.5th 531, 554, which held that employees have a bona fide interest in the confidentiality of their contact information, which is less sensitive information than one's financial data.) Therefore, Respondent has satisfied the first prong of the Hill test. Respondent has established an objectively reasonable expectation of privacy in his financial records. The California Supreme Court has held "[i]t cannot be gainsaid that the customer of a bank expects that the documents, such as checks, which he transmits to the bank in the course of his business operations, will remain private, and that such an expectation is reasonable" because the bank customer's expectation is that the matters he reveals to the bank will be used by the bank only for internal banking purposes. (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Court (1975) 15 Cal.3d 652, 657, citing Burrows v. Superior Court (1974) 13 Cal.3d 238, 243.) Therefore, Respondent has satisfied the second prong of the Hill test. (part 1 of 2)
